57. My Current Youtube Strategy 1-25-25.mp4 Let me start by saying this was a request from current inner circle members and I don't necessarily feel like super qualified to even talk about it. But I put it up to a poll and it was literally a hundred percent of you wanted to hear about this. So I'll talk about what I'm doing. This is a video on my current YouTube strategy and approach. Um, in like total revenue we've probably generated a little over half a million dollars so far since July. So it's been uh, about six and a half months. Call it for my current YouTube strategy. Okay, every single video for me has a big topic which for me, all I talk about on my channel, and this is literally it. I scrubbed every single video that did not talk about this. Only videos on my channel are videos that talk about how to hit million-dollar months. Okay. And although that is technically a super non-compliant claim, and I have to make sure that every single video, if you look in the description of my videos, have earnings disclaimers. Those are super FTC compliant. I make sure that I pepper in the fact that you have a 0. 1% probability to hit $10 million a year, let alone an even lower probability than that to hit $12 million a year by hitting million-dollar months. I just make sure that I hand down the lessons from at this current time I'm sitting here recording this video, the 40 different people who we've helped as an agency over the years, or that we've helped through our education company and consulting deals. Hit million dollar months. So long story short, every single video that I come out with is around a big topic or just like a big claim in general or a theme is. What some YouTubers have told me it's called. So the big topic or the theme, in my opinion, is where all the ideation to YouTube starts. It is super easy for me to come up with any video topic and just sit there and talk about it because I'm only talking about things that I know. I want to be really clear when I say this, okay? Like, I don't have any process at all to do like competitive research. There is not a single person I can think of or that I know that talks about hitting million dollar months or talks about a theme that is similar to my theme. There's a lot of people who talk about like marketing and sales and operations and like. scaling businesses, but they don't talk about it at the scale that I'm trying to talk about it and imply, you know, people can get to with the lessons that I'm handing down to them. In addition to that, like you guys got to keep in mind, I have. A decade, a literal decade, almost 11 years now of experience as a scaled advertiser. Like in my job that I had before I started my marketing agency, I was spending, like, 400 grand a month. You know, as an agency, like currently we spend, just shy of $2 million a month, give or take the month, maybe as low as like a high million. And, um, yeah, I mean, we accumulate an absolute metric fuckload of experience on a month over month basis. So, like, I see new lessons every single month. That are currently working. Things that used to work. That no longer work. Um, things that are consistent and that are like patterns amongst all these people. That hit million dollar months. Our biggest clients hit five plus million dollars a month. We have two clients in particular that do five and a half million and five million. Uh, one that's hit six million once or twice. You know, we never have anybody that goes above that. I never talk about stuff I don't know. I don't do competitive research— is the point I'm attempting to make, okay? I am exclusively talking from a perspective of experience on YouTube. That is it. I am in real time all the time, given that that is my literal business. That theme of my YouTube channel. Is my life, that is my soul, like purpose to exist— is helping people hit million dollar months. Okay, so from that perspective, every single thing in my day— from like the problems and solutions that my clients are facing as an agency that we work intimately with to my education company. lot of my highest level inner circle members want to do and achieve. They all want to hit million dollar months to the just regular students that I see that are like marketers or business owners that are training their marketers through our education products. I get constant exposure to like the intimate behind the scenes problems that you all face that either prevent you from hitting million dollar months or help you hit million dollar months or create momentum. Hit million dollar months like I have unlimited content ideas that are literally served up to me daily. I just write down things that come up. That's all I really do. You know. As things are working or as I see problems, I'm talking to somebody about— like an intimate conversation. I'm getting fed ideas. I just go and write down that. I later make content about. I do want to be clear when I say this. Because this is something that I found quite interesting. So, because I'm constantly talking from a perspective of experience and like unique, original thoughts. Like, I'm not out there just— I call these copy and paste personal brands. I think they're some of the funniest things that exist. These people have no original thought at all. It's actually really easy to spot somebody who's highly inauthentic and not talking about something that they actually fucking know about. Surprisingly to me as a new youtuber. This is what most fucking youtubers are doing, which is shocking to me. So I want to be really clear when I say this: This is not meant to be like a stupid, cheesy, you know, hippie buzzword. When I say, 'be authentic,' okay, but like you have to talk about stuff that you actually know. You cannot talk about stuff that you just see somebody else talking about, and then you fucking make like handy down content for it. When you have no fucking clue what you're talking about, you have to talk about stuff that you know. Okay and that you are like a master at that's the one thing that I think so far, from my YouTube experience, is like a differentiating factor. You know, I would imagine, if I was not and I was watching somebody like me, I would think it's a breath of fresh air to have somebody come along who you can tell actually gives a fuck about you making more money. And can give you very clear ways consistently, and damn near every single video on how to do just that. I don't think that you necessarily need to pick like a huge claim, but I do want to say this. I think that my big claim covers everything beneath that, like me talking about hitting million-dollar months consistently gets people that want to add another million dollars a month to their business. It consistently gets us in conversations with the people that I talk to in damn near every video, which are doing like a couple hundred thousand dollars a month. And want to hit a million dollars a month. It also gets me people who aspire to just like make more money in general and they're just like they want to be an agency owner and maybe make like 100k or like whatever small amount of money you know and they'll buy one of my education products too as a result of just me talking big. So I think there's like one key lesson here I want to make really clear: if you if you talk too small, you only get the things that are below that or like barely above that, you know? And you don't get the things that are above that unless you talk above that. So me talking at like a scaled level consistently from my like big theme of my channel gets everything beneath that naturally. Like, I don't have to make videos telling somebody how to make a fucking measly $100,000 a month like some of those turds on YouTube, dude. I get those. Like naturally, you can obviously assume if I'm talking about hitting a million dollars a month, that what I'm talking about is going to work for you. If you're trying to hit, like, that little, small fry number, you know what I mean? So that's my point. Is the bigger the conversation typically, the easier it is to clear out everything beneath that. That's one of the beneficial things I wanna make sure I hand down in this specific video. Again, I can't stress it enough. Although some of you are probably looking for some answer that makes you feel like you can just rapidly spin up ideas on the fly without having actual experience to sit there and talk about too, that's just not the case. This is the other important thing to note. I have so much that I can talk about, about each of the things that I'm sitting there talking about on YouTube, that it's also really easy for me to give a lot while still holding so much back, okay? So I do this system on YouTube. I call it the 90-10 system. So the 90-10 system for me is... Game where I hold back 90% of the fucking meat potatoes that I could otherwise drop and I give them 10%, and What's crazy is? The 10 that I drop is still so valuable to the people that watch my channel that so like sometimes we actually get an objection in our buying process where people are like, 'Dude, I just don't believe that your paid stuff can be better than what I find from YouTube from you.' And I'm like, 'Well, let me show you what we've got in our fucking content portal alone, let alone all the stuff that we do in real time on like weekly group calls, one-on-one calls, masterminds like we don't share any of that shit with the public, dude.' And it's important to note like. Even if you have the right offer, which I think I do. I could share damn near a hundred percent of what I actually know on YouTube, which I don't to be fair, but I could and I would still get a load of buyers as a result of that. Because it's important to note, like it's not just teaching people the 'what to do.' It's also holding them accountable. To the systems and the processes and the SOPs and the course content and the community of people they can ask questions to, hitting me up to ask questions like you'll notice if you go through my YouTube videos, like sometimes people ask questions. That are like questions that relate to the 'how,' and this is a big part of this as well, which is what not how. Okay. And I want to give a shout out to Russell Brunson on this specific tip from back in the day when I learned this from him. He would always teach this specifically to his webinar strategy where he'd be like, 'All right, in your webinars, you want to teach him a fuckload about what to do. But not how to do it.' That's a lot of what I do on YouTube. I just sit there and I tell people, 'This is what you need to be doing.' And this is what you need to be doing. And this is what you need to be doing. And then I constantly withhold— that this is the how you technically do it.' Okay and that's also a big part of, like, what makes an education company easy to get transactions for, and also for our marketing agency to get it easy to get transactions for. Is we're telling people stuff that, when they hear it, they're like, 'Holy fuck,' that makes a lot of sense. And I can see clear as day that that would make me more revenue as a result of doing it. But how the fuck do I do it? Try to seek out how to do it. And when there is nothing there on how to do it, that's publicly available, they're more than willing to transact. And then as soon as they transact, they realize like they stepped into this world. Where the 90-10 system exists, where they're like, 'Oh my God,' this guy's paid stuff is so much, somehow, so much more in-depth, and there's so... Much fucking more of it than what I just saw on YouTube, and I get access to the guy to help. Like, holy shit, that's a very consistent reaction that we get from everybody who buys literally anything that we sell, an education product or a marketing agency. So again, YouTube, our entire strategy, at least with our education company, depending on what you're doing, is what, not how, we withhold. Hold literally 90% of the fucking juice that we could otherwise put people on game to. And we're just dropping 10% of stuff that they still have never fucking heard before where they listen to it. And they're like, 'I mean, my God, that makes so much sense.' Nobody's fucking talking about that. And I love this guy's experience. Like I would love to do this stuff. The cool part as well. And this is also another little caveat is. I want to make people money. with the content. Okay, like the free stuff. This is another golden rule that I follow consistently. And it doesn't necessarily like if you don't have like a make money offer or something like that, which almost all of you do. If you don't, though, you would want to make sure that you follow this rule with get them whatever the result is, like get them some kind of result, like let them feel it. rather than just, you know, being like a viewer of your content. Like I like to give people stuff that they can go do that when they go do it, they make money from it. And they're like. I mean, my God, like I had somebody the other day that came to me and they said they got an extra $200,000 a month. Listen to that, right? They got an extra $200,000 a month from watching a handful of my YouTube videos and taking action on it. How easy do you think it was to close that person? They closed with us, just to be clear, for a $20,000 a month consulting deal. How fucking easy was that close? It was super easy because they had already made, dude, they're paying me 10% of what they made for free off of like a handful of my videos. You know how much more money they've already made paying us $20,000 a month? Like, the first month they added another $100,000 a month on top of that. $200,000 that they already made from the free stuff. That was in the first 30 days. So here's my point, right? I... genuinely give a fuck about making content that can make people money. Okay. Even though I'm withholding things. And this is also something that's important to understand. I am trying to be really valuable while also intentionally holding back a significant amount of value that I could otherwise sit there and talk about. That is the only thing that I would honestly say I do extremely intentionally and well. In my content, currently okay, and that also results, like, I understand how this game works in the education space. I give you a bunch of stuff that you could go do and that you could go and take action on that. If you do go take action on, like, yeah, it's going to be tough to learn it on your own and figure it out. But hey, if you do try it out, it's going to make you more money, even if you kind of fuck it up a little bit. And then, because you fucked it up a little bit and it still made you money, you're like, 'Wow, if it worked like this and I fucking piece mailed it together with the shit I learned on YouTube for free, what would I go and get from over here from the paid stuff?' OK, that's the kind of mindset you want to have. OK. I also pepper in consistent call to actions at like a one-third point in the video. So, if this is the entire video length right here, which always varies for me, I mean, they could be as low as like 20 minutes. I've had some videos that are like fucking two hours long. Okay. I just sit there and I talk about whatever the content is. Um, an unbound amount of time until I either get thirsty enough to just want to stop and go drink a little water or get hungry or like, you know, just feel like I finished whatever it is I need to talk about, about one third of the way through the video is typically when we find the average watch time is. Okay, so sometimes. between about a five to eight minute mark on average is when we consistently see our like average watch time is, no matter how long the video is. Okay, so as a result of that, we always try to pepper in some type of call to action to go and buy something, okay? So a lot of YouTubers, I constantly see them say what I think is kind of stupid, where they're like, oh, like, you know, like the video for me or comment. Like, I don't give a fuck about you liking the video or commenting. Like, I appreciate it, you know, and I'm thankful that you do that kind of stuff. But it's like, what I really give a fuck about is you giving me money. OK, that's what I actually give a fuck about, obviously, which is what I'd hope you give a fuck about, too. So around that average watch time threshold, I'm making sure that I pepper in— that call to action to go and buy something now. I could in almost every video pitch my agency services, but the agency services are like a very finite, even more finite availability for us to take on and service deals in comparison to our inner circle offer. And our inner circle offer is also finite. In terms of the quantity of people that we can allow in and the quantity of people that we can service, my master internet marketing program and my other education company services are technically something that's a lot more scalable and something that I should actively promote more. However, it's a transactional service. I have come to value recurring revenue, okay? I find recurring revenue is not only just for my own selfish benefit, but better because I get paid consistently month over month. I also find it's a lot better for the people that are doing the business with us. Someone just puts up a singular lump sum payment, in almost all instances, they give the most fuck at the beginning and the end when that transaction is going to come to an end or it's going to recur again on an annual basis. There's a lot of stuff in the middle that gets dropped, versus when somebody pays us on a month-to-month basis. Even if it's just an agency fee or an inner circle membership fee or whatever it is—okay. They have an incentive to give a fuck about making the money too. And I like to align incentives. I'm a big incentive person. I think incentives control almost all of our behavior on planet Earth for what we do as human beings. And a lot of smart people out there agree with me. That same logic, by the way. So from an incentive perspective, I go above and beyond to try to consistently pitch recurring products. Again, not just for my own selfish benefit. I'm not trying to virtue signal to you. The way. I obviously give a fuck about your money. I love your money. I appreciate your money. I love it coming in month over month. But again, from an incentives perspective, I also deeply give a fuck about you caring. Go out there and make the money with what you're spending on us. Okay. I want you to consistently get an ROI. I want you to be so above what you pay us in terms. Of the financial return that you generate, that you don't even think twice when that fucking recurring charge comes in, about how valuable it is and how happy you are to consistently pay us money. Okay? I am enthused. At the opportunity to help people make more cash. And I want them to be enthused to go out there and make that cash. And I have found, simply put, that recurring transactions make that the case. Again. I'm going to make sure that I pepper in around that average watch time, a call to action for one of our recurring offers is the point I'm trying to make. I am also going to make sure that I pepper in a call to action at the tail end of the video as well. And that call to action. At the beginning and at the end are both for money things. Okay, at this very very end, threshold here at the very very end, I am also typically peppering in a call to action to do things like subscribe to like the video and to comment like, do all that other shit and share it. The only other time that I do that is at the very beginning. OK, so all of my intros, if we just take a moment to comment on that real quick, are all very direct. If we looked at our YouTube stats together for like how it actually plays out, we found that. About two-thirds to half, give or take, what types of videos we're dropping on a month-over-month basis are returning subscribers. Two-thirds are returning subscribers. Half are returning subscribers, and then the other half— or the other one-third— give or take, what month-statistics we're looking at are new people who like, aren't currently subscribed. So at the beginning of almost every video, I'm like, 'Hey, what's going on, guys? Welcome into the video. My name's Jeremy Haynes. I talk about hitting million-dollar months, and this video is no different. In today's video, we're going to be talking about XYZ. If you're new to the channel, welcome. Welcome in. Again, just to reiterate, all we talk about around here is hitting million dollar months. If you find value in this video, go check out all my other videos and subscribe to the channel. That way you get notified anytime that we drop. More content that's going to help make you more money. And if you're already a subscriber, welcome back. I've got another banger for you. Let's dive in. And I try to keep the intros as short as humanly possible. Also try to make sure that I talk to both of those demographics I want to encourage people to subscribe and like, you know, join into the Jeremy Haynes world. But I also want to make sure sure that I'm talking to the existing subscribers since they're a majority or at least half. Give or take the video, just say, 'Hey man, like welcome back. You know, pleasure to have you back. Looking forward to helping you make more money in this video.' Let's dive right in. That's not just like a little strategy. You know, I do like legitimately, have like some enthusiasm and happiness about the fact that people are coming back and watching those videos. So I do want to say, 'What's up and thank you. But it's also a good strategy to just make sure you're actively talking to the two different types of people that are watching your community. I'm sorry, watching your content. You have two types of folks. Brand new folks who have never heard from you. That might be seeing that video for the first time—so you gotta like welcome in and say hello to. And you got the people that you're welcoming back, okay? So just make sure you pepper that in. All the rest of this should literally be content. So it's like all this content, all this content, and. I've found in my videos in particular, literally just as I'm doing now, my handy dandy iPad and just being able to visualize stuff is extremely helpful for me. There's a great book: Frogs into Princes by Richard Bandler. Richard Bandler teaches psychotherapists for a living. That's his version of an education company. And he is one of my favorite authors. ever and in that book frogs in a princess he introduces this system called the VAK visual auditory kinesthetic so there are two different ways you interpret this data right now okay number one is your input and number two is your output so as an example while you sit here and watch my videos i am a very visual expressor. OK, so like the way I like to communicate to other people is typically in a visual way. Right. That's why I've specifically picked an environment that looks good to film this. Video and instead of a random piece of shit like not well put together environment for you, okay that's why I have good hygiene because I give a fuck about how I look, that's why I visually think about how much I'm talking with my hands right now as I sit here and express myself to you. Think about how much I'm visualizing things on this iPad. I'm not doing that strategically for any other reason than just I'm trying to hit all three. I'm trying to not just use my own preferential way to communicate to others through visual preference. I'm also trying to make sure. Thank you. That obviously, you could just listen to my videos and still get value from it without having to see whatever it is that I'm drawing, okay? And in addition to that, kinesthetic. Kinesthetic people are like the people who can't sit still. I love using Kevin Harrington as an example here. If you ever get to be around Kevin Harrington, and shout out to Kevin. Kevin, in the instance that he ever watches this video, Kevin will never fucking sit still. Kevin is one of the most kinesthetic people I've ever seen. Like he's just always fucking tweaking out. Like I remember I was sitting down and. Event and I. I almost just started to ignore what was happening on the stage and I was just watching Kevin. Kevin was like putting his leg up on his knee and then he'd take it down like a second later and then he'd like— I could just like how he was sitting like this. And then, you know, he'd like adjust it again. And then, you know, he kind of hit one of these. And at first I was like, did this guy, is this guy fucking tweaking out? Like what is going on? You drink too much coffee. You know, dude, that's consistently how this guy is— everywhere he is, and no shit, he's as rich as he is, because kinesthetic people do extremely well when they're also money or success-oriented at getting a lot richer, because they're just constantly like the way they express themselves is through like action and physical movement. You know, and a lot of kinesthetic people there's a lot of like overly sensitive people in life that are very emotional, you know, and the way that they typically call themselves like empaths, as an example, or like things like this where the way that they choose to communicate are like in very physically expressive ways. You know, like if a kinesthetic person wanted to demonstrate to you that like they love you or something, they would use a lot of like physical touch and they would try to express it through a lot of emotion and like making sure you literally. Feel what it is that they're trying to express. Okay. So again, visual, auditory, and you learn that way and you express yourself to others that way. So my videos simply put— as you know—I'm not trying to overreach here and make it sound like I'm doing some fucking crazy advanced shit, but I do try to be very intentional about this because this is how you reach more people instead of just one type of those people. If I only did things in a very like auditory way, I would lose all of the visual people. I would lose all of the kinesthetic people. The way that I attack all three again is by having visuals for the visual folks, which is easy to do on YouTube. Of course, having the audio set up in a way where the people who are just auditory can listen to it, without having to watch watch it at 2x speed and still get all the value that they need from it. And then the kinesthetic people, I try to be like high energy. You know, I try to have like passion. And I try to inject emotion into what I'm communicating. That way, the kinesthetic people can also pick up what I'm putting down when I'm trying to express things in that particular way for that demographic. So again, V-A-K. So this specific. That you see here the intro, a lot of content, call to action for something that is recurring and money-oriented at that average watch time mark which you might need some data to accumulate to see what your threshold is. More content, a meet a majority of the content. And then that final call to action at the end there with another money thing. And then we close it out with the outro of, like, 'Hey, you know, like subscribe, go watch some of my other videos and all that fun stuff. Thanks for being here. So, okay. Consistent call to actions, I think, are a pretty big thing. Long story short, for me, I consider myself a busy, rich person. So the right team makes a big difference here. I actually have a set of people. First of all, I have my editors. And my editors, they do all of the video. And they also do the thumbnails. And they also do—everything, like, the text stuff, like the titles, the descriptions. In addition to that, they post it and then they send me the link. Okay. And they use this link, by the way, there's like a little pro tip. There's a few different tools like this, but this, this one in particular that they use. Whenever you share it on a social media platform and it's clicked, it takes them off of that specific social media platform and it drives them straight into the YouTube app. So this is called LinkTW.in. Okay. So I'll write it down here for you. LinkTW. in. And there was another one, by the way, that was used by one of my buddies, John. And John had shared with me. something that I thought was actually pretty cool. It was a similar tool, but long story short, the result of this tool, it was called URL Genie, but with a G. Uh, so U R L. G-E... And I. Dot us and this does the same exact thing as this link TW dot IM thing. Bye. Anyway, long story short. Both of these tools enable you to post something onto the social platforms and have it click to the YouTube app. Which is huge. You don't want people watching like in the browser of an Instagram or tweet or like wherever the fuck you're sharing the video from. You want it to literally open up the YouTube app. That helps with retention tremendously and it also helps with all your engagement statistics like them liking, commenting, sharing and all that other shit. Uh, big difference there, by the way. When you use those two, but anyway, my editors— so I pay these guys. And this, it varies, give or take the month. It's as low as three cam month and as high as five cam month. And the reason there's that variance is dependent upon the quantity of videos that are posted. Since we post twice a week, like some months are short, like February, is an example. Will be closer to the three cam month mark. Whereas, like, let's use the example. There's a month that has an opportunity for us to post, like 12 videos, you know, since there's a lot more days of the week where we might do the two-a-days. Just based on how the month is set up. That might be a month where I'm closer to like 4K. And then on top of that, I pay these guys for shorts. So I never film shorts directly. I'm just... not a fan of that. I find that you can clip a tremendous amount of shorts from all of my long form content. They still look like I shot them in a way where I was creating it for short form. So give it. Take the month and like— whether I want to post a fuckload of shorts or not, this is the caveat. By the way, I know we're talking about YouTube here, but like, I'm not that sold that shorts make like a difference to the quantity of revenue you get. I find that all the guys that I know that are like really into content and they like have done it for years and years, and And they've gotten a lot of reach and they get a lot of notoriety and in some instances even fame off of shorts and reels. And TikToks. I don't give a fuck about that. I mean, guys, I'm talking about hitting a million dollars a month. It's like an extremely niche topic. My content isn't going to go wide. You know, I'm not going to all of a sudden become like... super popular with the general public. You know, I actively talk poorly about the general public in a lot of my content, so it's quite the opposite. It's extremely niche, that's the point I'm trying to make. So, although yes, my reels do get like shares, they do get comments, they do get likes, you know, people watch them. The point I'm trying to make is like I prioritize YouTube above everything else. So these long-form pieces of content. That is specifically where I'm generating like all that extra revenue that we just talked about at the beginning of the video from. That's where I'm getting a lot of the people who consistently give us feedback, by the way, like, dude, I'm on. average watching like four to six of these videos we just did this mastermind this weekend at the uh here in miami for the inner circle and for all those who came in person like you remember this exercise for those who didn't i'll walk you through it i had everybody stand up who had bought from my YouTube specifically. And it was like half the room. Okay. And I asked a simple question. I was like, all right, guys, if it took you one video and you were like, this is my fucking guy. I bought after just one video. Go ahead and sit down. Like, literally, one person sits down. And that one video, by the way, was like two hours long just to just to comment on that. Then I was like, all right, if it took two videos, like go ahead and sit down. Like, literally, two people sit down. So now we got three people out of like, you know, fucking 40 that were still standing. And, um, Okay, so out of the 37 people that are left, I say, okay. If it took three videos, sit down, maybe like another handful. And then by the time we got to about five videos, that's when more than half the people were sitting down. OK, so on average, it took about five videos. Keep in mind— 20 minutes like an hour long per video— to get these people to sit down and say, 'Yep, I'm done. Okay now, from here, this is where I got a little crazy. Right. The other half. I just started naming like a higher count of videos. We got to like 10 and nobody was really sitting down. So I just had him start naming it off. I was like, 'Alright, how many videos did you watch, dude?' This was insane. Like some of them were like, I was watching, like, 15 to 20 something videos, and dude, no joke, like, the other collective half of them started sitting down around that particular range of content consumed, just to put it in perspective, and again, these are long, lengthy videos, for them to eventually convert on a couple thousand dollar a month inner circle membership for this specific group of people, and some of those people being consulting clients at, you know, tens of thousands of dollars per month. So anyway. I digress. The point I'm trying to make is YouTube is extremely valuable. I don't necessarily prioritize the shorts. I see all this, you know, I know we went a little bit off course here, but long story short, I pay my editors three to five K a month and some months it's a little more expensive because I do have them make shorts. Okay. Now, I have another team that is just for thumbnails. So although my original team does make thumbnails, I have a separate guy that works on like big channels. Allow me to explain how this works, okay? So at the very beginning of a YouTube video, what we've learned so far is there's like a consistent group of people that will go back, like the existing subscribers. You know, the people who are most enthusiastic about watching my content, like they'll go back and they'll always watch it first. And then. That. That data. is not necessarily accurately representing what people who don't know me yet. Will need to see, for the titles and thumbnails, to eventually convert later on. So just to give you some perspective, like one of the number one things all my editors and like YouTube people care about is the click-through rate on these videos. Okay, and they want to click the rate to be as high as possible. So although yes, my original thumbnails that I post are, in my opinion, still good. Well, my youtube Who I have a whole separate YouTube guy just to be clear. My YouTube guys like my YouTube Strategist. I guess is what you could call him. His name's Gabe, and I worked with Gabe back in the day on Dan Locke's account when we were going when we were growing Dan Locke to a couple million a month. And my YouTube strategist, again, his name's Gabe. He's in the inner circle with us, by the way. We've had him do some talks on YouTube growth. He got Dan Locke's channel to 4 million subscribers. And I don't even fucking know how many other channels Gabe's grown to. A tremendous amount of subscriber base. I'm not in any way, shape, or form optimizing around getting a tremendous amount of subscribers to be clear. But still, I take Gabe's perspective very seriously on the tips and tactics that he shares with me. What we do first is we let the normal editing team post the title and post the thumbnails, and we let that ride for about 72 hours. After 72 hours, we find that the video... starts to kind of like slow down on the initial subscriber base and like consistent viewers watching it. And then from there, it starts to get recommended in the YouTube home feed and like recommendation algorithm. And that is where we then pay the YouTube thumbnail guy to swap the thumbnail to something that appeals to people who don't know me yet. And that same YouTube strategist, Gabe, Gabe will come in and he'll adjust the title. And a lot of the titles that Gabe will pick are specifically. Reaching people who don't know me yet, okay, and also, just on like a secondary note, we call it search engine optimization, video search engine optimization, and digital real estate. We essentially want to claim certain videos to be our own, terms of like search terms, long term. Okay, I want to own specific search terms over a long term duration of time, and as a result, Gabe will go in and he'll adjust things like those little hashtags in the video that are behind the scenes, and some other, again, search engine optimization tactics to make that happen. Outside of the thumbnail strategy, this is essentially the three people that I pay. Collectively, on a month-over-month basis, between these three people, I think I end up collectively paying about 8K a month as like an average amount. And, that's not that bad to me because, keep in mind, like, I on a month, like, in the first month of July, we were already getting like, I don't know, 10K a month off of recurring revenue from new people who joined. There's some months, like, I'm filming this right now on the 21st of the month. And just this month, we've already had six people join, at $5,000 a month. So that's thirty thousand dollars in recurring revenue right there, on top of you know, in mind, like $8,000 a month's obviously already paid for, from the first month of recurring revenue, plus give or take. Actually, we're actually a little bit above $30,000 a month because we've had a few people as well that buy our Master Internet Marketing Program. So we're probably closer to around $50,000 a month, plus or minus. I'm not sure the exact number; I won't make it up. But we're right around that number for the month so far, just off of you know, get one to four thousand views per video on YouTube, which is pretty cool. That'll compound into a tremendous amount of additional revenue as time continues to go on. I'm sure, but yeah, long story short, the right team. Matters a lot— like I end up talking to these three sets of people quite frequently throughout the month. A lot more frequently than what I ever would have considered prior. This department in my business has become a real priority. They matter a lot to us. We want to make sure that we retain these people. We want to make sure that they feel good about working with us. There's a high probability that this 8K will go up through time, just as a result of... them continuing to make us more money, simply put. Okay, big topic, something you'd be authentic about, the ideation phase, like I'm just pulling from experience. I think that's the best place for everybody to pull from. You know, pulling from your experience, makes truly unique content. The 9010 system, give out 90%, I'm sorry, hold back 90%, give out 10%. The 90% should be reserved for the courses and the education products and whatever they're going to buy. Teach the 'what' not the 'how', make sure that you are making people money with the content before they ever spend money with you. It makes the transactions that you're asking for far easier. Consistent call to actions around that one third mark or whatever the average watch time is in your video. We went through the flow of how the videos look. I then just talked to you about my team structure and what we do overall inside of our staff. To make all of this functionally work, so that's really it. I mean, to be fair, there's not really much else I do. There's no like magic shit I'm over here using to, you know, come up. With random ideas you know, like competitive research and stuff, there's no like lengthy fucking teleprompters. And also, just to be clear, I'm filming everything in almost all instances on an iPad. The iPad has huge batteries and they shoot in 4K at 60 frames per second. They also have little stabilizers in it. I'm shooting on like a somewhat flimsy tripod. The only thing I will say is, inside that setup works fucking phenomenal. Outside, keep in mind, like I'm on a fucking balcony in the sky that has like a fuckload of wind. That's rocking that little tripod around, and that can create some image stabilization issues. And I have to fix that from like a quality perspective. Either just film inside all the time, or get a sturdier tripod, I guess, right? Besides that, it's like I'm using those little Rode microphones, like those little square ones that hook up to the iPad. I had to buy a special cable to get them to hook up, to be clear, a little USB-C cable sold by Rode. Um, Rod, you can buy them at Best Buy. I don't know, 200 to 300 bucks. I have my second iPad. This is just the Notes app, to be clear, and an Apple pencil. And then I just do the, you know, screen recording thing. That's like usual to most Apple products. I'm not doing anything. I don't have like a video guy behind the scenes sitting there filming with me, anything like that. Um, I usually just film it like random times when I kind of feel like it. That's the one, that's the one other tip. Like I don't have a consistent time where I'm like, 'All right, this is the day and time I'm sitting down filming.' I never really liked that. You know, I got to be like in the mood to do it. That's another big thing. I have watched some people's content over the years. Cause, like, obviously, I've just watched shit on YouTube myself. Sometimes I feel like it's really easy to tell that somebody's just having like a bad time or they're not in the zone, you know? I only like to film content when I'm in the mood. Like sitting here filming right now today, you know, I... paid to film in this particular place. If I didn't give a fuck to film at this exact time right now, or if I didn't feel like doing it, I wouldn't do it. I would have happily paid the money and just not fucking come. You know, I want to make sure I film when I actually feel good, simply put. That's a big thing that I think. Also separates out my content being a little better. So, yeah, anyway, I mean, hey, maybe this will turn out to be one of those like Mr. Beast type videos where, you know, I film again in like fucking 10 years and, you know, I got infinitely more subscribers and I make infinitely more money than I do off this now.
